table.name: Name
level.L0.name: Traditional
level.L1.name: AI-Assisted
level.L2.name: AI-Enhanced
level.L3.name: AI-Optimized
level.L4.name: Agentic / Autonomous
level.L0.description: No structured approach. Manual processes dominate. No AI tooling adoption.
level.L1.description: Beginning to adopt AI tools. Pilots underway (<20% adoption). Basic automation exists.
level.L2.description: Significant AI adoption (50%+). Comprehensive automation. Self-service emerging.
level.L3.description: Wide AI adoption (75%+). AI agents for complex tasks. Predictive capabilities.
level.L4.description: Full AI integration. Multi-agent systems. Self-healing with minimal human intervention.
level.L0.evidence: Observation of manual workflows, absence of automation tools
level.L1.evidence: License counts, pilot team lists, initial metrics
level.L2.evidence: Adoption dashboards, automated workflow logs, self-service portal
level.L3.evidence: AI usage reports, agent task logs, predictive analytics dashboards
level.L4.evidence: Autonomous operation logs, multi-agent coordination evidence, MTTR metrics
gap.default_action.h1: Define the baseline and establish coverage metrics.
gap.default_action.h2: Expand the pilot to more than 50% of teams; instrument OKRs.
gap.default_action.h3: Reach universal coverage and continuous optimization.
effort.s.label: Small
effort.m.label: Medium
effort.l.label: Large
effort.xl.label: Extra-Large
wizard.placeholder: [Customize {field} via the Implementation Guide Wizard — currently using template defaults]
overview.intro: This document provides a comprehensive explanation of the scoring methodology used in the {org} AI Maturity Assessment, including detailed justifications for each capability score and the weighted average calculations that determine pillar and overall maturity levels.
overview.purpose.1: Provide full transparency on how each score was determined.
overview.purpose.2: Document the evidence base used for scoring decisions.
overview.purpose.3: Explain the weighted average calculation methodology.
overview.purpose.4: Justify the rationale behind each maturity level assignment.
overview.purpose.5: Enable stakeholders to understand and validate assessment results.
methodology.levels.intro: The AI Maturity Framework uses a 5-level scale (L0-L4) to measure capability maturity. Each level has specific characteristics and evidence requirements.
methodology.guidelines.intro: The following principles were applied consistently during scoring:
methodology.guidelines.1: Score based on EVIDENCE, not aspirations — always require proof.
methodology.guidelines.2: When capabilities span multiple levels, score at the LOWER level.
methodology.guidelines.3: Consider CONSISTENCY across teams — isolated excellence does not raise the org score.
methodology.guidelines.4: Platform Engineering [PE] capabilities indicate Agentic Platform Engineering readiness.
methodology.guidelines.5: Use half-points (e.g. 1.5) only when strong evidence supports partial progress.
methodology.formula.intro: Each capability has an assigned weight (2 or 3) based on its strategic importance. The Pillar score is calculated as:
cross_survey.intro: This section consolidates complementary signals collected through the developer and learning surveys. Use these data to validate leadership-declared maturity against real behavior, capacitation demand, and adoption evidence.
cross_survey.developer_maturity.desc: D2-D8 scores calculated by deterministic rubric from the anonymous Developer Survey.
cross_survey.developer_insights.desc: Aggregated report with Copilot adoption, practices, governance, anonymized pain points, and recommendations.
cross_survey.learning_plan.desc: Identified roadmap with demanded topics, cohorts, Champions, mentoring, and workshop calendar.
pe.interp.not_ready: Significant foundational work needed before adopting either IDP path. Focus on standardization, source-of-truth, basic CI/CD.
pe.interp.low: Basic capabilities exist. Close fundamental gaps before scaling. A small Open Horizons (Backstage) pilot may be valuable to learn before committing.
pe.interp.medium: Foundation is in place. Open Horizons is the lower-risk default; Three Horizons fits organizations already in the Red Hat / OpenShift ecosystem.
pe.interp.high: Either path can be chosen with confidence. Decision is strategic (vendor preference, OSS vs commercial, team capacity).
evidence_sources.intro: The scoring above is supported by evidence collected during assessment sessions. The categories below list the kinds of artifacts and observations that informed the rationale.
evidence_sources.empty: No evidence sources were captured for this assessment.
conclusion.p1: {org} has reached an overall maturity of L{score} ({level}) across the assessed pillars. This represents a meaningful baseline from which to drive systematic improvement toward the target state.
conclusion.p2: {high_gaps} capabilities currently sit at HIGH gap priority and warrant immediate attention. Pillars analyzed: {pillars_summary}. Each capability has been scored against quantifiable evidence and validated against the framework rubric.
conclusion.p3: The companion Transformation Roadmap documents (Parts 1-4) translate this current-state picture into a phased H1/H2/H3 plan with specific initiatives, success metrics, and governance structure to close every gap identified here.
roadmap.exec_summary.intro: This document presents the transformation roadmap for {pillar_name} as part of {org}'s AI-Native Software Delivery Maturity transformation journey. The roadmap outlines the strategic initiatives, effort, and timeline required to evolve from the current pillar maturity (L{current}) to the target level (L{target}) over a 36-month period.
roadmap.three_horizons.intro: The transformation unfolds across three time horizons — H1 (Foundation, Months 0-6), H2 (Scale, Months 6-18), and H3 (Transform, Months 18-36) — enabling progressive capability building while delivering incremental value at each stage.
resources.tech_intro: The technologies below are recommended over the 36-month roadmap. Effort tier indicates the implementation cost of each — see legend below.
impl_guide.exec_summary.intro: This Implementation Guide provides the cross-cutting governance, phasing, resource plan, change management, and quick wins structure required to execute the AI transformation defined for {org} across all three pillars.
impl_guide.transformation_overview: The transformation covers {total_caps} capabilities across {pillars_count} pillars over a 36-month horizon split into three phases (H1: Foundation, H2: Scale, H3: Transform).
impl_guide.governance.intro: The governance structure ensures alignment between strategic intent, working-group execution, and pillar-level outcomes. Three layers of cadence cover monthly executive review, weekly program-level coordination, and weekly pillar-level operations.
governance.steering.purpose: Sets strategic direction, approves major investments, and resolves cross-pillar conflicts.
governance.tpo.purpose: Coordinates execution across pillars, tracks risks and dependencies, and drives the program timeline.
governance.working_group.purpose: Executes initiatives within {pillar_name} and reports progress to the TPO.
raci.legend: R = Responsible · A = Accountable · C = Consulted · I = Informed
phase_0.intro: Phase 0 establishes the foundational structures required before initiative execution begins in Horizon 1.
phase_0.objective_1: Establish governance structures (Steering Committee, TPO, Working Groups).
phase_0.objective_2: Approve the transformation roadmap and effort allocation.
phase_0.objective_3: Identify pilot teams and pilot capabilities.
phase_0.objective_4: Define and instrument baseline KPIs for the program.
phase_0.success_1: All governance bodies have named members and confirmed cadence.
phase_0.success_2: Roadmap approved by Executive Steering Committee.
phase_0.success_3: Pilot teams identified and onboarded to the transformation.
role.program_director.scope: End-to-end program ownership; reports to Steering Committee.
role.lead_architect.scope: Cross-pillar architecture coherence and technical decisions.
role.pmo_analyst.scope: Status reporting, risk register, dependency tracking.
hiring.intro: Sequence of hires required across the transformation. Roles starting in earlier months are critical-path.
procurement.intro: Technology procurement is sequenced to align with horizon-specific capability needs. Effort tier reflects implementation cost — see legend in §1.
procurement.leverage_intro: The transformation should leverage tooling already in use by the organization wherever possible to reduce risk and deployment time.
adkar.intro: ADKAR (Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, Reinforcement) provides the change-management framework for stakeholder transitions across the transformation.
quick_wins.intro: The first 90 days produce visible momentum. The activities below are designed to be completable, demonstrable, and to build organizational confidence in the program.
quick_wins.default.w1_4.1: Communicate transformation roadmap to all engineering teams.
quick_wins.default.w1_4.2: Stand up the Steering Committee and TPO with first cadence meetings.
quick_wins.default.w1_4.3: Identify 3 pilot teams for Horizon 1 initiatives.
quick_wins.default.w5_8.1: Begin first 1-2 H1 initiatives in pilot teams.
quick_wins.default.w5_8.3: Run first round of training for pilot teams.
quick_wins.default.w9_12.1: Demonstrate first measurable productivity uplift in pilot teams.
quick_wins.default.w9_12.2: Communicate pilot results to wider engineering organization.
quick_wins.default.w9_12.3: Plan Horizon 1 expansion to additional teams.

evidence.none_text_captured: No evidence text was captured. Score derived from question responses only.
empty.technology_investments: Technology investments will be detailed in the next planning iteration. Use the H1 Initiatives section above as the starting point.
empty.team_skills: Team and skills allocation pending. Coordinate with the TPO to populate this section based on the H1 Initiatives scope.
empty.effort_summary: Effort summary pending. Effort tier per horizon will be derived from the technology and team plans once they are finalized.
empty.success_metrics: Pillar-specific success metrics will be defined in the next planning iteration. Use the per-capability H1 success metrics in section 2.1 as the starting baseline.
empty.risks: Risk register pending. Coordinate with the TPO to identify pillar-specific risks during program kickoff.
empty.next_steps_30d: Immediate actions pending. Use the per-capability H1 initiatives above as the starting point for the first 30-day plan.
empty.next_steps_h1: H1 launch activities pending. These will be defined as the H1 detailed plan is finalized.
empty.dependencies: Cross-pillar dependencies will be mapped during the program kickoff phase.
empty.h1_initiatives: H1 initiatives for this capability are pending. Coordinate with the strategic roadmap engine to generate recommendations.
ape.explainer.title: About these paths
ape.explainer.intro: Agentic Platform Engineering is the destination — an internal developer platform with self-service golden paths, AI-augmented workflows, and autonomous operations. It admits two implementation paths:
ape.path.three_horizons.description: Three Horizons (with Red Hat Developer Hub): commercial, OpenShift-aligned, fully supported. Best fit for organizations already in the Red Hat ecosystem or requiring vendor-backed enterprise support.
ape.path.open_horizons.description: Open Horizons (with Backstage): open-source, vendor-neutral, fully customizable. Best fit for organizations preferring OSS, with strong platform engineering capacity, or requiring cloud-agnostic deployment.
ape.note.same_destination: Both paths produce the same end-state. The choice is operational (tooling, vendor relationship, support model), not capability-based.
ape.note.h_phasing: H1/H2/H3 time phasing (Months 0-6 / 6-18 / 18-36) applies to both paths.
